director.summary: Week 20 (ending 19 May 2026) shows a concerning trend across all three stores with overall sales down 6.0% (Palmerstown), 7.0% (Balbriggan), and flat +0.2% (Skerries). The pattern indicates weather/seasonal headwinds rather than operational failure—produce is down 14.6%, 14.1%, and flat respectively, suggesting cool/wet conditions suppressing fresh categories. Skerries significantly outperforms peers despite similar pressures, posting strong growth in edible grocery (+9.6% YTD) and modest weekly decline. Palmerstown and Balbriggan face deeper structural challenges with meat margin erosion (-2.99% and -3.79%), deli weakness (-18.8% at Skerries, -3.6% Palmerstown, -2.5% Balbriggan), and bakery underperformance (-9.0%, -7.0%, -6.5%). Margin deterioration across serveover meat and critical margin categories poses the biggest threat to profitability.

stores.name: Palmerstown
stores.summary: Dublin commuter store down 6.0% weekly (-€22k), reflecting weak lunchtime trade and margin compression in meat (-2.99 pp) and bakery. Own-brand solid at -6.7% but impulse grocery cratering at -7.3%. YTD -5.4% indicates consistent underperformance. Critical weakness in pork serveover (-26.3%) and prepared produce (-10.8%) suggests category mix issues or promotional erosion.

stores.name: Balbriggan
stores.summary: North Dublin family store down 7.0% weekly (-€23k), worst performer. Severe lamb serveover collapse (-31.5%), beef serveover decline (-8.3%), and chicken weakness (-13.0%) signal pricing or quality perception issues. Deli meats down 38.4%, prepack discontinued. Waste profile (1.1% vs peer average 1.2%) suggests demand-driven destock rather than spoilage. YTD -3.3% shows recent deterioration.

stores.name: Skerries
stores.summary: Coastal village store near flat weekly (-€0.2%, only -€868), best resilience despite -6.5% bakery and -18.8% deli decline. Edible grocery +9.6% YTD and produce near-flat demonstrate strong basket discipline. Fruit sales +15.4% counter produce overall softness. Waste low at 1.1%, margin profile stable. Tourist/summer seasonality likely to drive recovery; execution solid.

drivers.text: Skerries edible grocery +9.6% YTD and fruit +15.4% weekly show seasonal uptick and strong promotional elasticity in defensive categories.
Fish serveover at Palmerstown +38.3% weekly (+€477) signals successful promotion or category refresh; leverage model across estate.

Meat department margin collapse: Palmerstown -2.99 pp, Balbriggan -3.79 pp on total category. Serveover pork particularly weak (Palmerstown -26.3%, Skerries -26.0%). Indicates aggressive competitor pricing or internal cost increase not passed through.

Bakery down 6.5–9.0% across all stores; scratch bakery down 15.5% at Palmerstown. Chilled fruit crash -24.4% (Palmerstown), -25.0% (Balbriggan) confirms weather headwind (cold = fruit demand down, hot food up, but hot food margin weak).

Deli & Food to Go structural decline: Skerries -18.8% weekly, Balbriggan deli meats -38.4%, Palmerstown prepack -45.7%. Prepack category discontinued at Balbriggan/Skerries suggests margin/waste crisis on ambient food-to-go.
Transaction count down 9.7–15.1% at Palmerstown/Balbriggan 10am–11am (lunchtime core), indicating footfall loss in commuter peak. Bank holiday or school holiday calendar shift should be verified.

Own-brand penetration declining: Palmerstown -6.7%, Balbriggan -3.3%, despite category focus. Private label margin (22.5–22.8%) not offsetting branded decline, suggesting consumers trading down to value lines or competitors' house brands.

priorities: Stabilise meat serveover margin (pork, lamb, chicken) – audit competitive pricing & cost variance immediately.
Investigate lunchtime footfall collapse (Palmerstown/Balbriggan) – calendar, roadworks, competitor opening, or loss of corporate accounts.
Deli & Food to Go: kill or fix prepack line; refresh hot food range to boost margins & volumes.
Weather-proof produce & bakery: confirm cold/wet forecast; prepare seasonal bundle promos (soups, stews, baked goods).
Replicate Skerries' edible grocery success (fresh/chilled buying) across Dublin stores within 2 weeks.

leaks.title: Meat Serveover Margin Bleed
leaks.impact: €600–900 / week per store (2–3% margin loss on €5–6k weekly category sales)
leaks.fix: Immediate audit: (1) Compare cost of goods vs. last year same week; (2) Review promotional cadence vs. Dunnes/Tesco; (3) Check portion/yield specs in training; (4) If cost-driven, escalate to buying; if promotion-driven, reposition premium lines and reduce depth discounts on beef.

leaks.title: Deli Prepack Discontinuation & Ambient Food Crisis
leaks.fix: (1) Assess shelf life & waste data from past 8 weeks; (2) If waste >15%, do not resume; (3) If demand/margin issue, pivot to fresh deli sandwiches or hot prepack (baked goods, not chilled); (4) Train deli to upsell 'made today' messaging.

leaks.title: Chilled Fruit Seasonal Cliff
leaks.fix: (1) Confirm weather (cold spell forecast to break by late May); (2) Prepare summer berry campaigns (Signature berries up-sell at Skerries); (3) Reduce import fruit intake; (4) Bundle chilled fruit with summer salads/Greek yogurt cross-promotions by week 21.

leaks.title: Palmerstown/Balbriggan Lunchtime Footfall Loss
leaks.fix: (1) Pull foot traffic data from door counter for past 4 weeks, compare to calendar (bank holidays, school breaks); (2) Check Waze/Google traffic near store; (3) Canvass corporate accounts (banks, offices) for churn; (4) If no calendar shift, run competitor mystery shop; (5) Consider lunch bundle promo (meal deal) if footfall is truly lost, not deferred.

leaks.title: Own-Brand Volume Leakage Despite Margin Focus
leaks.impact: €4,600–8,000 / week YTD (own-brand qty down 3–11%, sales down 1–7%, but margin not recovering margin gap)
leaks.fix: (1) Category-level analysis: which own-brand SKUs driving decline? (confectionery, dairy, grocery?); (2) Compare own-brand price delta vs. leading competitor; if >2%, reposition; (3) Check shelf visibility & signage at Palmerstown (lowest performance); (4) Trial premium own-brand line (Signature range) to offset volume.

urgent.text: Pull meat department cost variance report (YoY, this week) vs. selling prices by line (pork, beef, lamb, chicken, fish) by EOD; escalate to buying if cost >2% adverse.
Audit deli prepack waste data (Balbriggan/Skerries last 8 weeks); if >20%, do not re-list; if <15%, trial small restock Mon/Wed/Fri with fresh baking angle.
Confirm Week 20 calendar (bank holiday, school break, GAA/sporting event) vs. footfall variance; cross-check vs. competitor promotions and own email blast/ loyalty data.

today: Circulate meat serveover pricing audit request to store managers (pork, beef, lamb line-by-line, cost & RRP vs. LY same week); due 9am tomorrow.
Request weather forecast & confirmed chilled fruit intake plan for week 21–22 from supply chain; brief traders by 2pm.
Pull top 10 own-brand SKUs by loss (Palmerstown, Balbriggan) and run shelf audit (visibility, pricing, signage) this afternoon; photo evidence required.
Book call with Palmerstown store manager to review 10–11am foot traffic data (door counter) past 4 weeks and validate calendar/competitor factors by 3pm.
Email all three stores: confirm deli strategy (prepack Y/N, hot prepack focus, made-fresh messaging) by EOD; gather any local insight on customer feedback.
Schedule analyst deep-dive on Skerries edible grocery +9.6% success factors (promotions, category mix, supply timing) for replication brief Wed morning.

compliance.text: Food Safety: Deli prepack discontinuation may indicate shelf-life or labelling issue; confirm HACCP review completed and document any corrective action taken before any re-listing.

Promotions Governance: Meat serveover margin collapse may reflect undocumented or unapproved promotional activity (e.g., store-level markdown beyond corporate approval). Audit promotional authority and POS override logs at Palmerstown/Balbriggan.

cross.title: Produce–Bakery–Deli Weather Cascade
cross.why: Cold/wet weather historically drives hot food (bakery, soup, coffee) UP and fresh produce DOWN. Current data shows produce -14.6% (Palmerstown), bakery -9.0%, deli -3.6%. This counter-intuitive pattern (hot food also down) suggests weather alone is not the driver—possible loss of tourist/weekend footfall at Skerries and corporate lunch volume at Dublin stores.
cross.action: Segment sales by daypart (lunchtime vs. evening, weekday vs. weekend) for past 4 weeks. If weekend/tourist soft, confirm summer holiday patterns and front-load summer promos (BBQ, picnic bundles) to Skerries for week 21. If lunch soft, execute corporate account recovery calls by Friday.

cross.title: Margin Architecture Stress: Own-Brand & Serveover Both Failing
cross.why: Palmerstown own-brand down 6.7% (qty -11.2%), meat serveover margin -2.99 pp, bakery scratch bakery down 15.5%. Margin expansion via own-brand and premium food-to-go should be offsetting volume decline, but both are simultaneously weak. Suggests pricing power loss across categories, not category mix issue.
cross.action: Conduct competitive price benchmark (Dunnes, Tesco, local competitors) on 5 key SKUs in each department (own-brand, meat serveover, bakery) by Thursday. If Moriarty is 2%+ higher on own-brand, reposition; if higher on meat, confirm quality/freshness perception with shopper feedback.

cross.title: Skerries Resilience Playbook
cross.why: Skerries flat weekly (-0.2%) while Dublin stores down 6–7%, despite similar product mix and market conditions. Edible grocery +9.6% YTD, fruit +15.4% weekly, waste low at 1.07%, own-brand stable at -1.2%. Indicates superior promotional execution, supply discipline, or community loyalty.
cross.action: Export Skerries promo calendar, supply spec (freshness lead times, local sourcing %), and loyalty redemption rate to category teams. Trial top 3 Skerries promos at Palmerstown/Balbriggan in weeks 21–22. Survey Skerries customers on top 3 reasons for visit to benchmark against Dublin stores.

cross.title: Transaction Elasticity & Lunchtime Peak Vulnerability
cross.why: Palmerstown/Balbriggan transaction count down 181–172 units (9–15%) in 10–11am slot; average spend also down €0.80–0.96. Suggests simultaneous loss of shopper count AND basket size in commuter prime time. Skerries shows flat transactions & spend, indicating no footfall pressure.
cross.action: Map Palmerstown/Balbriggan lunchtime promotions vs. Skerries equivalent week. If Skerries running stronger lunch bundles, replicate immediately. If not, run urgent discount test (meal deal or bundle) Mon–Fri week 21 and measure transaction lift. Link back to any corporate account/office openings/closures in catchment.

diagnosis.wrong: Across-the-board margin collapse in meat serveover (especially pork, lamb, chicken) combined with structural deli discontinuation and lunchtime footfall loss at Dublin stores suggests neither weather nor promotion but rather a competitive pricing shock or loss of customer traffic from offices/corporate accounts.
diagnosis.why: If weather were the sole driver, hot food (bakery, deli, coffee) should be UP. Instead, all three categories are DOWN. Skerries' resilience (flat sales, stable margin, strong edible grocery) despite identical weather proves the issue is localised to Dublin stores. Lunchtime transaction collapse (-15% at Palmerstown 10–11am) points to commuter footfall loss, not basket size weakness. Meat margin bleed across all three stores (even Skerries) indicates either (a) competitor opened/expanded with aggressive pricing in North Dublin/Dublin region, or (b) corporate cost increase (e.g., labour, supply chain) that corporate margin policy has not yet authorised pass-through on.
diagnosis.fix: Immediate action plan: (1) Audit Palmerstown/Balbriggan competitive pricing on beef, pork, lamb vs. Dunnes/Tesco by EOD Thursday; (2) Pull Palmerstown/Balbriggan foot traffic data (door counter, loyalty app logins) for past 6 weeks, overlay with calendar (bank holidays, school breaks, competitors' ad spend) to confirm footfall loss is real and date-correlated; (3) If footfall confirmed lost, execute corporate account outreach (call top 20 office parks/banks) to identify churn and run retention bundle promo; (4) If competitor pricing confirmed aggressive, submit emergency repricing request for beef/pork/lamb to buying; interim mark-down on top 3 SKUs allowed within margin tolerance (max 1.5 pp loss authorised); (5) Restart deli prepack only if waste <15% and margin >25%; otherwise shift resources to made-fresh sandwiches and hot prepack to recapture deli revenue; (6) Brief all three store managers on summer seasonal opportunity (Skerries playbook) and lock down week 21 promotional calendar by Friday.

waste.summary: Total waste across the Moriarty Group for the week of 2026-05-19 reached €3,847.86, with Palmerstown accounting for €1,324.48, Balbriggan €1,156.64, and Skerries €1,366.74. Produce categories (fresh herbs, vegetables, salads, and fruit) are the primary waste drivers across all stores, followed by dairy and prepared meals. Critical ordering and demand forecasting failures are evident, particularly with high-value items showing minimal retail sales despite significant waste quantities.
waste.summary_paragraph_2: Markdown dependency is particularly severe in Balbriggan where butter products (Connacht Gold, Avonmore) show 33-16 units wasted with only 3-5 units sold at full price, indicating chronic overstock. Palmerstown and Skerries demonstrate similar patterns with specialty products like fresh mozzarella, organic salmon, and prepared meals being ordered in excess of store demand. Immediate action required on product ordering protocols, shelf-life management for perishables, and demand-led purchasing to prevent further margin erosion.

stores.name: Palmerstown

top_offenders.product: 1755585 000 SUPERVALU FRESH IRISH BUFFALO STYLE MOZZARELLA | 600GRM
top_offenders.insight: CRITICAL ORDERING FAILURE: 30 units wasted with only 1 unit sold at retail. This high-value specialty cheese has zero markdown usage. Immediate action: reduce standing order by 90%, implement demand forecasting review, consider removing from permanent range if demand cannot support stocking.

top_offenders.product: 1608093 000 FILL TO THE BRIM €5.25 PREPARED MEAL | 1 PCE
top_offenders.insight: TOTAL SELL-THROUGH FAILURE: 20 units wasted, zero retail sales. This meal has no customer traction. Action: discontinue ordering immediately, review shelf placement and visibility, or replace with alternative prepared meals that have proven sales velocity.

POSITIVE PATTERN: Despite 23 units waste, strong retail sales of 53 units indicate this is a legitimate fresh produce item with expected spoilage. However, waste-to-retail ratio of 4:10 is high. Action: review delivery frequency, shift to 300g packs, or reduce weekly order by 15%.

HIGH-VALUE WASTE WITH ZERO SALES: 15 units completely wasted, suggesting either expired stock or display issues. Action: audit current stock immediately, implement date-rotation protocols, consider switching to pre-packed salads with longer shelf-life.

top_offenders.product: 1637220 001 SV FRESH IRISH CHICKEN FILLET PORTION | 1KGM
top_offenders.insight: MARKDOWN AVOIDANCE PROBLEM: High waste value (€166) with minimal retail sales (10 units) and zero reductions suggest poor sell-through and no promotional action taken. Action: implement automated markdown protocols at 48-hour mark, review packaging size (1kg may be too large), reduce daily order quantity.

stores.category_breakdown: Produce (mushrooms, spinach, apples, pears, grapes, broccoli) accounts for approximately 35% of Palmerstown's waste. Dairy and specialty items (mozzarella, prepared meals) represent 28% of waste value despite lower unit counts due to high price points. Bakery items (batch rolls, pasta) show reasonable sell-through rates relative to waste. Meat products (chicken, lamb kofta, duck) demonstrate strong value waste (€530+) with insufficient retail sales, indicating ordering disconnected from store demand patterns.
stores.action: PRIORITY 1 - Implement daily order review for high-value items (mozzarella, chicken fillets, prepared meals) with retail sales data mandatory before ordering. PRIORITY 2 - Activate automatic 50% markdown at 48-hour mark for all fresh produce to prevent waste escalation. PRIORITY 3 - Audit standing orders on specialty items (organic spinach, buffalo mozzarella) and reduce by 40% pending 3-week sales trend analysis.

stores.name: Balbriggan

top_offenders.insight: SEVERE MARKDOWN DEPENDENCY FAILURE: 33 units wasted with only 5 retail sales and ZERO reduction markdowns applied. Store is choosing to waste €95 rather than reduce price. Action: eliminate standing butter orders immediately, implement 3-for-2 promotional strategy, shift to smaller 200g packs, audit competitor butter pricing.

MIXED SIGNAL: High waste (45 units) but strong retail sales (54 units). This appears to be a legitimate fresh salad with inherent spoilage. However, waste represents 45% of volume. Action: reduce order frequency from daily to 5x weekly, implement end-of-day markdown protocol at 20% to clear aging stock.

MARKDOWN AVOIDANCE - PREMIUM SIZE FAILURE: 16 units wasted with only 3 retail sales. The 454g format is too large for Balbriggan customer base. No reductions applied. Action: discontinue 454g format immediately, stock only 227g, implement 25% markdown on any remaining oversized units today.

POSITIVE MARKDOWN DISCIPLINE: 28 waste units but 18 reductions applied and 62 retail sales shows active inventory management. Waste-to-sales ratio of 31% is acceptable for fresh salads. Action: maintain current ordering but monitor daily to ensure reductions triggered at optimal time windows.

top_offenders.product: 1699269 000 ROSIE & JIM GF GARLIC KIEV TRAYPACK | 300GRM
top_offenders.insight: COMPLETE ORDERING MISMATCH: 17 units wasted with only 1 retail sale. Gluten-free prepared meal has no customer demand at Balbriggan. Action: cease all orders immediately, review customer demographic data, remove from planogram, reallocate shelf space to proven sellers like chicken fillets.

stores.category_breakdown: Dairy (butter, cheese) dominates Balbriggan waste at 43% by euro value, with systematic failure to apply markdowns on premium formats. Fresh salads and produce account for 32% of waste but show better retail sales conversion, indicating inherent perishability rather than ordering failure. Prepared meals (soups, Kiev) represent 18% waste with near-zero sell-through, suggesting severe demand forecasting disconnect. Bakery items perform well relative to waste indicating appropriate stock levels.
stores.action: PRIORITY 1 - IMMEDIATE: Halt all butter orders (all formats) for 2 weeks; conduct customer demographic analysis to determine if premium butter range is appropriate for Balbriggan store size (374 units). PRIORITY 2 - Remove all prepared specialty items (Rosie & Jim, Happy Pear creamy soups) from standing orders and implement 30-day trial periods with daily P&L review before restocking. PRIORITY 3 - Implement mandatory markdown protocol: salads at 48hrs/20% off, perishables at 72hrs/30% off to prevent waste accumulation.

stores.name: Skerries

top_offenders.insight: ACCEPTABLE FRESH HERB WASTE: Despite 32 units wasted, strong retail sales of 81 units indicates this is expected spoilage for fresh herbs. Waste-to-sales ratio of 28% is normal for herbs. Action: maintain current ordering but implement twice-daily stock rotation, relocate to high-traffic area if not already, consider promotional bundling with other herbs.

NEAR-BALANCED BUT CONCERNING: 30 units wasted with 31 retail sales indicates near 50% waste rate. This prepared hot item requires tighter production scheduling. Action: shift to on-demand preparation model, implement 4-hour shelf-life limit with mandatory markdowns at 2-hour mark, reduce batch sizes by 30%.

CRITICAL ORDERING FAILURE: 20 units wasted with only 1 retail sale. Multi-pack format not suited to Skerries customer base. Action: discontinue apple trays immediately, shift to loose apples (single piece) which show better sell-through, audit all multi-packs for similar underperformance.

top_offenders.product: 1552293 003 THE HAPPY PEAR CREAMY ROOT VEG SOUP | 400GRM
top_offenders.insight: ZERO DEMAND PREPARED MEAL: 9 units wasted with only 1 retail sale across two stores (also in Balbriggan). This artisanal soup brand has no traction. Action: remove from all three stores immediately, identify 5 alternative ready-meal suppliers with proven sales history in comparable stores.

COMPLETE FLOWER RANGE DISCONNECT: 11 units wasted with zero retail sales. Flowers require daily demand that Skerries (424 units) cannot sustain. Action: discontinue flowers entirely or reduce to 2x weekly flower deliveries on Thu/Sat only, focus on patio plants which show positive sales (Palmerstown: 5 units sold).

stores.category_breakdown: Produce and fresh herbs account for 38% of Skerries waste (basil, apples, strawberries, cherry tomatoes) with mixed results - herbs show strong sales while packaged fruits (apple trays) show zero traction. Prepared meals and specialty items (soups, pestos, flowers) represent 28% of waste with near-total sell-through failure, indicating category mismatch with store demographics. Meat/deli products (chicken fillets, bacon, ham) show 22% waste with reasonable retail sales, suggesting portion-size or production-volume calibration issues. Non-perishable items (pasta, breadcrumbs) perform well relative to waste.
stores.action: PRIORITY 1 - Audit entire 'specialty prepared meals' and 'artisanal products' category (Happy Pear soups, pestos, flowers) across all three stores; discontinue items with less than 3:1 retail-to-waste ratio. PRIORITY 2 - Shift Skerries ordering to 'smaller format, higher velocity' model: single-piece produce instead of multi-packs, limit prepared hot items to on-demand production, reduce specialty cheese range by 40%. PRIORITY 3 - Implement store-specific demand models: Skerries clearly favors convenience items (batch rolls, sausage rolls, loose items) over premium/specialty; recalibrate planograms accordingly.

group_actions: DEMAND FORECASTING OVERHAUL: Implement store-specific demand models using 12-week sales history. All three stores show ordering disconnected from retail sales velocity. Require category managers to validate orders weekly against actual Retail Qty data before placement.

MARKDOWN PROTOCOL STANDARDIZATION: Establish automatic markdown triggers: 48hrs old = 25% off, 72hrs old = 50% off, 96hrs old = donation/waste. Balbriggan's butter waste demonstrates systematic failure to apply markdowns. Non-negotiable implementation across all stores with daily compliance reporting.

SPECIALTY/PREMIUM CATEGORY RATIONALIZATION: 'The Happy Pear' soups, premium butter formats, gluten-free specialty items, and flowers show zero-to-minimal sell-through across stores. Conduct immediate SKU audit and remove bottom 15% of prepared meals, specialty items, and premium formats. Reallocate shelf space to proven high-velocity items (batch rolls, sausage rolls, loose produce showing 80%+ retail sales).

FRESH PRODUCE ORDERING CALIBRATION: Herbs, salads, and vegetables show 25-45% waste rates across stores. Shift to 5x weekly delivery cycles instead of daily for all fresh produce, implement end-of-day markdown protocol (20% at 6pm, 40% at 8pm), and reduce initial order quantities by 25% pending 3-week stabilization period.

PERISHABLE PRODUCTION CONTROLS: Hot prepared items (breaded chicken, rotisserie chicken) and baked goods show 25-30% waste rates. Implement on-demand production model: produce to order based on hourly sales history, set 4-hour maximum shelf-life with mandatory 50% markdown at 2-hour mark. Track production waste separately from ordering waste.

CROSS-STORE PRODUCT HARMONIZATION: Buffalo mozzarella (wasted 30 units in Palmerstown, 12 in Balbriggan) and Happy Pear soups show consistent failure across multiple stores, indicating brand/product category mismatch with store demographics. Establish single 'hold' list of products failing across 2+ stores pending 30-day removal review. Prevent repeat ordering of proven non-sellers.
